name: org.machanism.machai:ghostwriter
description: Ghostwriter is a Java library and command-line application in the Machai ecosystem for AI-assisted repository automation, guided documentation maintenance, and reusable act-driven workflows. Based on the generated Javadoc for Ghostwriter 1.2.0-SNAPSHOT, the project provides filesystem-oriented processors that inspect project layout metadata, discover source, test, documentation, and module directories from ProjectLayout, scan matching files or folders, extract embedded @guidance directives through format-specific reviewers, and execute prompt episodes against repository content using a configured Genai provider. The library centers on org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.GuidanceProcessor for guidance-based review flows and org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor for TOML-defined act execution with inheritance, prologue and epilogue related acts, configurable episode order, and external act catalogs. It also exposes a standalone CLI via org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.Ghostwriter for command-line startup, configuration loading, argument parsing, and project scan orchestration. Ghostwriter includes a reviewer SPI in org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer with built-in implementations for Java, Markdown, HTML/XML, TypeScript, Python, PlantUML, and generic text guidance files, enabling extension to additional artifact formats. The org.machanism.machai.gw.tools package provides host-integrated function tools for reading, writing, patching, and listing files; executing validated commands with bounded logs and deny-list security checks; retrieving web content and invoking REST endpoints with selector, timeout, charset, and authentication support; managing act execution and episode navigation; processing guidance-tagged files; and maintaining project-scoped context variables. This combination makes Ghostwriter suitable for developer tooling, repository governance automation, documentation generation, prompt orchestration, extensible AI-assisted review pipelines, and host applications that need controlled local and remote tool execution around project-aware software engineering workflows.

usageContext: Embedding Ghostwriter in Java applications to automate repository-aware maintenance and review workflows
Running project scans that extract embedded @guidance directives from supported source and documentation file formats
Executing TOML-defined Act workflows with reusable prompt episodes, inheritance, and custom act directories
Building command-line automation for repository updates, documentation generation, and guided refactoring assistance
Extending file analysis support by implementing the Reviewer SPI for additional artifact types
Integrating host-controlled file, command, web, REST, context, and episode-management tools into GenAI-driven engineering platforms

constructors.description: Creates an act processor that runs Ghostwriter in Act mode. The processor uses the supplied project root directory as the base for relative paths, reads project layout metadata, initializes a configured Genai provider, and prepares execution of TOML-defined acts against matching repository files or folders.

examples.description: Creates an Act processor for the current project so a host application can run reusable act workflows against repository content.

constructors.description: Constructs a guidance processor that scans a project tree, resolves reviewer implementations by file extension, extracts @guidance directives, and dispatches the resulting prompts to a configured Genai provider.

examples.description: Instantiates the guidance-driven processor used when an application wants to scan repository files for embedded instructions rather than execute a named act.

constructors.description: Creates the file-system function tool registrar used to expose project-relative file reading, writing, patching, directory listing, and recursive discovery capabilities to a host-integrated GenAI runtime.

examples.description: Constructs the file tool set before registering it with a host-managed AI provider so workflows can safely interact with project files.

constructors.description: Creates the command tool registrar responsible for validated command execution, persisted log retrieval, regex-based log searching, bounded output capture, and process-control integration in a host environment.

examples.description: Instantiates the command tool set so a host application can attach secure shell command functionality to AI-assisted workflows.

constructors.description: Creates the web and REST function tool registrar that provides HTTP GET retrieval, selector-based extraction, plain-text conversion, authenticated REST invocation, and configurable request headers for AI-driven workflows.

examples.description: Builds the web integration tool set so a host can let Ghostwriter-assisted workflows retrieve documentation pages and call remote REST services.

features.name: main
features.signature: public static void main(java.lang.String[] args) throws java.io.IOException, org.apache.commons.cli.ParseException
features.description: Starts the Ghostwriter CLI, parsing command-line arguments, loading configuration, selecting the processing mode, and orchestrating project scans for automation and guided repository workflows.

examples.description: Illustrates programmatic invocation of the CLI entry point. In normal usage, developers run the packaged Ghostwriter application from the command line.

features.name: loadAct
features.signature: public static void loadAct(java.lang.String name, java.util.Map<java.lang.String, java.lang.Object> properties, java.lang.String actsLocation) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: Loads an act definition into a property map, supporting basedOn inheritance, built-in classpath acts, user-defined act directories, and child overrides of parent act properties.

examples.snippet:
java.util.Map<java.lang.String, java.lang.Object> properties = new java.util.HashMap<java.lang.String, java.lang.Object>();
org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor.loadAct("help", properties, "./acts");
examples.description: Shows how a host application can load an act definition before execution so tooling can inspect or customize reusable workflow properties.

features.name: setAct
features.signature: public void setAct(java.lang.String act) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: Selects an act definition and applies it as the current execution defaults. The method accepts the form "<name> [prompt]", allowing applications to choose a workflow and inject a concrete user prompt into the act inputs template.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor processor = new org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor(new java.io.File("."), null, "OpenAI:gpt-5.1");
processor.setAct("task Rewrite the README for new users");
examples.description: Demonstrates a practical act selection flow where a host application chooses a reusable task template and provides a user-level request string.

features.name: perform
features.signature: java.lang.String perform(java.io.File projectDir, java.io.File file) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: Reviews a file and returns a formatted fragment for the Ghostwriter pipeline. Implementations target specific formats and extract actionable @guidance instructions, returning null when no relevant guidance is present.

examples.snippet:
org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer reviewer = new org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.MarkdownReviewer();
java.lang.String result = reviewer.perform(new java.io.File("."), new java.io.File("README.md"));
examples.description: Shows how a host application can directly use a reviewer implementation to extract a prompt fragment from a Markdown file before passing it into a larger workflow.

features.name: executeCommand
features.signature: public java.lang.Object executeCommand(java.lang.String command, java.util.Map<java.lang.String, java.lang.String> properties, java.lang.String dir, int tailResultSize, java.lang.String charsetName, java.io.File projectDir, org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator configurator) throws java.io.IOException
features.description: Executes a system command in a controlled host environment with project-root confinement, deny-list security checks, configurable environment variables, bounded output buffering, and persisted command logs for later inspection.

examples.description: Represents the practical starting point for enabling validated shell command execution in a host application that embeds Ghostwriter tools.

features.name: getWebContent
features.signature: public java.lang.String getWebContent(java.lang.String url, java.util.Map<java.lang.String, java.lang.String> headers, int timeout, java.lang.String charsetName, boolean textOnly, java.lang.String selector, java.io.File projectDir, org.machanism.macha.core.commons.configurator.Configurator configurator)
features.description: Fetches web page content using HTTP GET or a file URL, with optional authentication, configurable headers, timeout, charset handling, CSS selector filtering, and plain-text extraction for documentation-oriented workflows.

examples.description: In a host-integrated setup, this tool registrar is used so AI workflows can fetch documentation pages or selected remote content as part of an automation task.

customizations.name: Reviewer
customizations.description: Primary service-provider extension point for adding support for new file formats that can contain embedded guidance directives. Developers implement this interface to teach Ghostwriter how to inspect additional artifact types and convert them into prompt fragments.

examples.description: Shows how to extend Ghostwriter with a custom reviewer for JSON files so repository-specific formats can participate in guidance extraction.

customizations.name: setActsLocation
customizations.description: Customization point for selecting an external directory that contains act definition files, allowing teams to maintain organization-specific workflow catalogs separately from built-in acts.

examples.description: Demonstrates how to configure a custom acts directory before selecting and executing act workflows.

customizations.name: setDisableNormalOrder
customizations.description: Controls whether normal episode execution should continue after handling a requested episode, enabling integrations that need explicit workflow sequencing rather than default progression.

examples.description: Shows a practical customization for integrations that externally manage episode transitions and do not want default follow-up execution.

studs.name: org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer
studs.description: Service-provider interface for components that inspect project files, detect embedded @guidance instructions, and return normalized prompt fragments for downstream processing. It is the main extension contract for adding new reviewable artifact types to Ghostwriter.

examples.description: Illustrates how a host application can discover reviewer implementations and inspect which file extensions they support before starting a scan.

examples.title: Install Ghostwriter as a Maven dependency and run a reusable Act workflow
examples.scenario: Embed Ghostwriter in a Java application that executes act-driven repository automation against the current project.
steps: 1. Add the Maven dependency org.machanism.machai:ghostwriter:1.2.0-SNAPSHOT to your project.
2. Ensure a Java 8+ runtime is available and choose a compatible Genai provider key such as OpenAI:gpt-5.1.
3. Create an org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.ActProcessor using the project root directory, a configurator, and the provider key.
4. Optionally call setActsLocation with a directory such as ./acts if your team maintains custom act definitions outside the built-in catalog.
5. Call setAct with a practical request such as task Rewrite the README for new users.
6. Execute the surrounding workflow so Ghostwriter scans matching paths and runs the selected act episodes.
examples.description: Practical how-to-use scenario for embedding Ghostwriter as a library: install it as a dependency, configure an optional acts directory, and launch a concrete repository workflow.

examples.title: Run the Ghostwriter CLI from a packaged jar
examples.scenario: Use Ghostwriter as a ready-to-run command-line application for guided repository automation.
steps: 1. Build or obtain the packaged Ghostwriter jar for version 1.2.0-SNAPSHOT.
2. Make sure Java is installed and available on PATH.
3. Open a terminal in the target project directory that should be scanned.
4. Run the CLI entry point using either the main class or the executable jar packaging.
5. Pass the scan target and any required runtime configuration expected by your distribution.
6. Review the updated files, logs, or generated outputs after the command finishes.
examples.description: Step-by-step usage example for the standalone CLI. The Javadoc identifies org.machanism.machai.gw.processor.Ghostwriter as the command-line entry point for starting Ghostwriter workflows.

examples.title: Extend Ghostwriter with a custom Reviewer implementation
examples.scenario: Add support for a new file type so Ghostwriter can extract guidance from additional project artifacts.
steps: 1. Create a class that implements org.machanism.machai.gw.reviewer.Reviewer.
2. Implement getSupportedFileExtensions so Ghostwriter knows which suffixes should be routed to your reviewer.
3. Implement perform(java.io.File projectDir, java.io.File file) to inspect the file and return a prompt fragment when actionable guidance is present.
4. Register the implementation using Java ServiceLoader metadata in your build or distribution.
5. Add the extension jar to the runtime classpath used by Ghostwriter.
6. Run a Ghostwriter scan and verify that files with your custom extension are processed by the new reviewer.
examples.description: Practical extension scenario showing how to add a new guidance-aware file format to Ghostwriter using the Reviewer SPI.

examples.title: Register file, web, and command tools in a host-integrated workflow
examples.scenario: Build an application that combines local file operations, remote documentation retrieval, and validated shell execution around an AI-assisted engineering workflow.
steps: 1. Add Ghostwriter to your build and initialize the host-side GenAI provider used by your application.
2. Instantiate org.machanism.machai.gw.tools.FileFunctionTools, org.machanism.machai.gw.tools.WebFunctionTools, and org.machanism.machai.gw.tools.CommandFunctionTools.
3. Register these tool sets with the provider so the workflow can access project files, fetch documentation pages, and run validated local commands.
4. Use the file tools to read or patch project artifacts, the web tools to fetch documentation or API reference content, and the command tools to run diagnostics or build-related commands.
5. Feed the gathered context into your Ghostwriter-guided prompt or act execution.
6. Review the produced outputs, logs, and repository changes to confirm the end-to-end automation result.
examples.description: Practical how-to-use example for host integrations that want Ghostwriter to operate with controlled access to local files, remote web content, and shell commands.
